summary: Paul Graham argues that starting a startup is a viable third path for students but that graduating changes incentives by removing the “student” escape hatch, making failure feel real and motivating. He explains why mid‑twenties founders often do best, highlighting young founders’ advantages (stamina, poverty, rootlessness, co‑founders, ignorance) and the biggest pitfall of student teams: building “class projects” instead of real, user‑driven products.
key_points: Graduation removes the social excuse for failure, increasing pressure and motivation to make a startup work.
Young founders benefit from stamina, low burn rate, mobility, easy access to co‑founders, and ignorance that counters intimidation.
Class‑project thinking fails because it solves fake problems and optimizes for effort rather than user value.
Work experience mainly teaches the real relationship between work, money, and user focus, not specific technical skills.
To prepare, learn by working at startups, choose co‑founders you respect, and build user‑focused software while in school.
money_quotes: For nearly everyone, the opinion of one's peers is the most powerful motivator of all—more powerful even than the nominal goal of most startup founders, getting rich.
Starting a startup is so hard that it's a close call even for the ones that succeed.
Poverty implies you can live cheaply, and this is critically important for startups.
The market doesn't give a shit how hard you worked.
When you know nothing, you have to reinvent stuff for yourself, and if you're smart your reinventions may be better than what preceded them.
tone: analytical, candid, and pragmatic with reflective moments
key_insight: The decisive edge for student founders isn’t just talent but understanding real users and incentives—graduate when you must, but avoid class‑project thinking and use your youth’s low burn rate and co‑founder access to build something people genuinely need.
